Gaussian Mixture Model Site Selection on SARS-CoV-2 Spike Protein Shannon Entropy Distributions: Robustness to Monotone Transformations

Vadim Tyuryaev, Jane Heffernan, Hanna Jankowski

2026-05-04

1 Overview

Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clustering is applied to per-site Shannon entropy vectors derived from two SARS-CoV-2 spike protein datasets — GISAID and NCBI — to identify sites exhibiting elevated evolutionary variability(Fraley and Raftery 2002; Cover and Thomas 2006; Shannon 1948).The primary goal of this analysis is not site detection per se, but rather to assess whether applying monotone transformations to the entropy distribution prior to GMM fitting provides any additional information or improvement in site selection, and whether the added layer of methodological complexity is warranted.

A natural question is whether the pronounced right-skewness of the entropy distribution, characteristic of genomic surveillance data where the majority of sites are highly conserved, adversely affects GMM fitting and subsequent site selection. To investigate this, six transformations are applied to the entropy vectors and the resulting site selections are compared with those obtained on the original scale. Five of the six transformations are monotone increasing — \(\log(1+x)\), \(\log(1+\log(1+x))\), \(\sqrt{x}\), Box-Cox (\(\lambda = -0.05\)), and logarithmic \(\ln(x)\) — and are therefore expected to preserve rank-based site selection. The reciprocal \(\frac{1}{1+x}\) is monotone decreasing and is included as a deliberate contrast: by inverting the entropy ranking it provides a natural negative control, confirming that site selection depends critically on the preservation of rank ordering. While monotone increasing transformations preserve rank ordering, Gaussian mixture models depend on the metric structure of the data — including inter-point distances and component variances — and are therefore not guaranteed to yield identical cluster assignments under such transformations(McLachlan and Peel 2000; Hennig 2015). Despite this, empirical comparisons indicate that, although monotone increasing transformations alter the geometry of the data, they do not materially affect the identification of high-entropy sites in the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein datasets considered here, and the additional methodological complexity they introduce is not justified.

Document structure and scope of analysis The complete entropy distributions (all 1273 sites) are visualised initially to illustrate the characteristic right-skewed profile of genomic sequence variability, where most positions exhibit near-zero entropy and a small subset displays elevated variability(Korber et al. 2020). All subsequent analysis — including GMM fitting, transformation experiments, and comparison tables — is conducted on filtered entropy vectors with zero-entropy sites removed. These positions carry no variability signal and therefore do not contribute to clustering. Additionally, singleton-entropy sites (observed in the NCBI dataset) are excluded, as they are likely dominated by rare events or sequencing artefacts rather than sustained evolutionary variation.

For each entropy vector, six sub-vectors are analysed: the complete filtered vector and five trimmed subsets — Q1+, median+, mean+, Q3+, and the 90th percentile+. This constitutes a sensitivity analysis of GMM-based site selection to the choice of pre-filtering threshold.

Distributions visualization Throughout this document, input entropy distributions are rendered in blue (#4682B4) and GMM-selected site distributions in green (#2CA25F).

2 Libraries and Helper Functions

library(ViralEntropR)
library(kableExtra)
library(dplyr) 
library(mclust)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)
library(here)

source("gmm_site_selection.R")
source("generate_comparison_table.R")

3 Output Directory

TABLE_DIR <- here("analysis", "GMM_transformation_study", "tables")
dir.create(TABLE_DIR, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
table_path <- function(filename) file.path(TABLE_DIR, filename)

4 Reference Sites

VOC-defining mutation sites are extracted from the sarscov2_variants package dataset. Two reference sets are used: NCBI comparisons use Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta (the VOCs present in the October 2021 NCBI dataset); GISAID comparisons additionally include Omicron, which is represented in the GISAID dataset through April 2024.

data("sarscov2_variants", package = "ViralEntropR")

extract_voc_sites <- function(labels) {
  sort(unique(unlist(lapply(labels, function(label) {
    idx <- which(sarscov2_variants$WHO_Label == label)
    if (length(idx) == 0L) {
      warning(sprintf("WHO_Label '%s' not found in sarscov2_variants.", label))
      return(integer(0L))
    }
    sarscov2_variants$Mutation_Sites[[idx]]
  }))))
}

voc_labels_ncbi  <- c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta")
voc_labels_gisaid <- c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta", "Omicron")

important_sites_ncbi  <- extract_voc_sites(voc_labels_ncbi)
important_sites_gisaid <- extract_voc_sites(voc_labels_gisaid)

sprintf("NCBI  reference sites (Alpha/Beta/Gamma/Delta): %d",
        length(important_sites_ncbi))
#> [1] "NCBI  reference sites (Alpha/Beta/Gamma/Delta): 40"
print(important_sites_ncbi)
#>  [1]   18   19   20   26   69   70   80   95  138  142  144  156  157  158  190
#> [16]  215  222  241  242  243  258  384  417  452  478  484  494  501  516  570
#> [31]  614  655  681  701  716  950  982 1027 1118 1191
sprintf("GISAID reference sites (Alpha/Beta/Gamma/Delta/Omicron): %d",
        length(important_sites_gisaid))
#> [1] "GISAID reference sites (Alpha/Beta/Gamma/Delta/Omicron): 64"
print(important_sites_gisaid)
#>  [1]   18   19   20   26   67   69   70   80   95  138  142  144  145  156  157
#> [16]  158  190  211  212  215  222  241  242  243  258  339  346  371  373  375
#> [31]  384  417  440  446  452  477  478  484  493  494  496  498  501  505  516
#> [46]  547  570  614  655  679  681  701  716  764  796  856  950  954  969  981
#> [61]  982 1027 1118 1191

5 Custom Transformation Objects

All transformations are monotone on the entropy support, ensuring rank ordering is preserved. Cluster membership may still change when the number of components G selected by BIC differs between scales; the comparison tables quantify this empirically.

loglog1p1p_trans <- scales::trans_new(
  name      = "log1p(log1p)",
  transform = function(x) log(log(x + 1) + 1),
  inverse   = function(x) exp(exp(x) - 1) - 1,
  breaks    = scales::log_breaks()
)

reciprocal_trans <- scales::trans_new(
  name      = "reciprocal(1+x)",
  transform = function(x) 1 / (x + 1),
  inverse   = function(x) 1 / x - 1,
  breaks    = scales::pretty_breaks()
)

boxcox_trans <- function(lambda = -0.05) {
  scales::trans_new(
    name      = sprintf("Box-Cox(\u03bb=%.2f)", lambda),
    transform = function(x) if (lambda == 0) log(x) else (x^lambda - 1)/lambda,
    inverse   = function(x) if (lambda == 0) exp(x) else (lambda*x + 1)^(1/lambda),
    breaks    = scales::pretty_breaks()
  )
}

6 Data

6.1 Loading

GISAID_US_aligned_feature_matrix_1273aa and Data_Matrix_Filtered_NCBI_Data_US are fully post-processed data matrices containing United States sequences only. The preprocessing steps for the NCBI dataset are documented in the preprocessing_pipeline package vignette (vignette("preprocessing_pipeline", package = "ViralEntropR")); the GISAID pipeline is recorded in analysis/GISAID_data_preprocessing/GISAID_Preprocessing_Pipeline.Rmd.

Each matrix has rows = sequences, columns = integer-encoded amino acid sites 1–1273 plus Date and Country metadata columns.

AL_df_GISAID <- readRDS(here("analysis","GISAID_data_preprocessing", 
                             "GISAID_US_aligned_feature_matrix_1273aa.rds"))
AL_df_NCBI   <- readRDS(here("data-raw", "NCBI_US_unaligned_feature_matrix_1273aa.rds"))

nr_gisaid <- nrow(AL_df_GISAID)
nr_ncbi   <- nrow(AL_df_NCBI)

nc_gisaid <- ncol(AL_df_GISAID)-2 # Date and Country
nc_ncbi   <- ncol(AL_df_NCBI)-2   # Date and Country

sprintf("GISAID: %d sequences x %d sites", nr_gisaid,nc_gisaid)
#> [1] "GISAID: 129371 sequences x 1273 sites"
sprintf("NCBI:   %d sequences x %d sites", nr_ncbi,nc_ncbi)
#> [1] "NCBI:   109536 sequences x 1273 sites"

6.2 Entropy Vectors

Entrop_Vec_GISAID <- apply(AL_df_GISAID[, 1:1273], 2, calculate_entropy)
Entrop_Vec_NCBI   <- apply(AL_df_NCBI[,   1:1273], 2, calculate_entropy)

sprintf("GISAID entropy range: [%.6f, %.6f]",
        min(Entrop_Vec_GISAID), max(Entrop_Vec_GISAID))
#> [1] "GISAID entropy range: [-0.000000, 1.560311]"
sprintf("NCBI   entropy range: [%.6f, %.6f]",
        min(Entrop_Vec_NCBI), max(Entrop_Vec_NCBI))
#> [1] "NCBI   entropy range: [-0.000000, 0.696367]"

6.3 Zero and Singleton Removal

sngl_gisaid <- -(
  (1/nr_gisaid) * log2(1/nr_gisaid) +
  ((nr_gisaid-1)/nr_gisaid) * log2((nr_gisaid-1)/nr_gisaid)
)
sngl_ncbi <- -(
  (1/nr_ncbi) * log2(1/nr_ncbi) +
  ((nr_ncbi-1)/nr_ncbi) * log2((nr_ncbi-1)/nr_ncbi)
)

sprintf("GISAID — zero-entropy sites:      %d", sum(Entrop_Vec_GISAID == 0))
#> [1] "GISAID — zero-entropy sites:      1"
sprintf("NCBI   — zero-entropy sites:      %d", sum(Entrop_Vec_NCBI == 0))
#> [1] "NCBI   — zero-entropy sites:      293"
sprintf("NCBI   — singleton-entropy sites: %d",
        sum(Entrop_Vec_NCBI == round(sngl_ncbi, 6L)))
#> [1] "NCBI   — singleton-entropy sites: 122"
sprintf("GISAID   — singleton-entropy sites: %d",
        sum(Entrop_Vec_GISAID == round(sngl_gisaid, 6L)))
#> [1] "GISAID   — singleton-entropy sites: 0"
Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S <- Entrop_Vec_GISAID[Entrop_Vec_GISAID != 0]

Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S <- Entrop_Vec_NCBI[
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI != 0 &
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI != round(sngl_ncbi, 6L)
]

sprintf("GISAID — sites retained after zero removal:             %d",
        length(Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S))
#> [1] "GISAID — sites retained after zero removal:             1272"
sprintf("NCBI   — sites retained after zero + singleton removal: %d",
        length(Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S))
#> [1] "NCBI   — sites retained after zero + singleton removal: 858"

7 Complete Data — Distribution Overview

The entropy distributions of the complete (unfiltered) vectors are shown here for both datasets to illustrate the characteristic right-skewed profile. No GMM is fitted to the complete data; all subsequent analysis uses the zero/singleton-removed vectors.

plot_entropy_distributions(Entrop_Vec_GISAID, prob = 0.9,
                           dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 complete (all 1273 sites)",
                           bins = 10)

plot_entropy_distributions(Entrop_Vec_NCBI, prob = 0.9,
                           dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 complete (all 1273 sites)",
                           bins = 10)

8 Untransformed Analysis (Zeros/Singletons Removed)

GMM is fitted on the zero/singleton-removed entropy vectors. The component with the highest mean entropy is selected as the high-variability cluster.

8.1 GISAID

GISAID_gmm_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S,
  prob             = 0.9,
  transfr          = NULL,
  hist             = TRUE,
  calc_gmm_orig    = TRUE,
  calc_gmm_transfr = FALSE,
  dataset_label    = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed",
  bins             = 20
)

tbl <- save_orig_comparison_table(
  GISAID_gmm_no0S,
  table_caption   = "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, untransformed",
  save_path       = table_path("NonTransformed_GISAID_no0S.html"),
  important_sites = important_sites_gisaid
)
tbl

8.2 NCBI

NCBI_gmm_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S,
  prob             = 0.9,
  transfr          = NULL,
  hist             = TRUE,
  calc_gmm_orig    = TRUE,
  calc_gmm_transfr = FALSE,
  dataset_label    = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed",
  bins             = 20
)

tbl <- save_orig_comparison_table(
  NCBI_gmm_no0S,
  table_caption   = "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, untransformed",
  save_path       = table_path("NonTransformed_NCBI_no0S.html"),

)

9 Transformation Experiments

All transformations are applied exclusively to the zero/singleton-removed vectors. For each transformation, the GMM is fitted on the transformed scale; selected sites are reported back on the original entropy scale for interpretability.

9.1 Box-Cox Lambda Exploration

\[ x^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} & \text{if } \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt] \ln(x) & \text{if } \lambda = 0 \end{cases} \]

where \(x > 0\) is the per-site Shannon entropy and \(\lambda\) is the transformation parameter. For \(\lambda\) close to zero the Box-Cox transformation approximates the natural logarithm, providing near-logarithmic compression of the right tail while remaining defined at \(x = 0\) in the limit as \(\lambda \to 0\).

Nine equally spaced values of λ across [−2, 2] are applied to the zero/singleton-removed vectors to identify which value most effectively yield the most symmetric shape of the distribution.

boxcox_transform <- function(x, lambda) {
  if (lambda == 0) log(x) else (x^lambda - 1) / lambda
}
lambdas <- seq(-2, 2, length.out = 9L)

plot_boxcox_grid <- function(ev, dataset_label) {
  plots <- lapply(seq_along(lambdas), function(i) {
    lam  <- lambdas[i]
    data <- boxcox_transform(ev, lam)
    ggplot2::ggplot(data.frame(x = data), ggplot2::aes(x = x)) +
      ggplot2::geom_histogram(fill = "steelblue", colour = "white",
                              alpha = 0.85, bins = 20L) +
      ggplot2::labs(title = sprintf("\u03bb = %.2f", lam),
                    x = "Transformed entropy", y = "Count") +
      ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 9) +
      ggplot2::theme(plot.title       = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold",
                                                               hjust = 0.5),
                     panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank())
  })
  print(
    patchwork::wrap_plots(plots, ncol = 3L) +
      patchwork::plot_annotation(
        title = sprintf("Box-Cox transformation \u2014 %s", dataset_label),
        theme = ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(
          face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 12)))
  )
}

plot_boxcox_grid(Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, "GISAID (zeros removed)")

plot_boxcox_grid(Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S,   "NCBI (zeros and singletons removed)")

9.2 Log1p

GISAID_log1p_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = scales::log1p_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_log1p_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = scales::log1p_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_log1p_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Log1p",
  table_path("Log1p_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_log1p_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Log1p",
  table_path("Log1p_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

9.3 Log1p∘Log1p (Double Compression)

GISAID_loglog_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = loglog1p1p_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_loglog_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = loglog1p1p_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_loglog_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Log1p\u2218Log1p",
  table_path("LogLog_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_loglog_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Log1p\u2218Log1p",
  table_path("LogLog_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

9.4 Square Root

GISAID_sqrt_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = scales::sqrt_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_sqrt_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = scales::sqrt_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_sqrt_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Square root",
  table_path("Sqrt_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_sqrt_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Square root",
  table_path("Sqrt_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

9.5 Reciprocal

GISAID_recip_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = reciprocal_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_recip_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = reciprocal_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_recip_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Reciprocal",
  table_path("Reciprocal_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_recip_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Reciprocal",
  table_path("Reciprocal_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

9.6 Box-Cox (λ = −0.05)

bc_trans <- boxcox_trans(lambda = -0.05)

GISAID_bc_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = bc_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_bc_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = bc_trans,
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_bc_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Box-Cox (\u03bb = \u22120.05)",
  table_path("BoxCox_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_bc_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Box-Cox (\u03bb = \u22120.05)",
  table_path("BoxCox_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

9.7 Log (natural log)

GISAID_log_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_GISAID_no0S, transfr = scales::log_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "GISAID \u2014 zeros removed", bins = 20
)

NCBI_log_no0S <- gmm_site_selection(
  Entrop_Vec_NCBI_no0S, transfr = scales::log_trans(),
  prob = 0.9, hist = TRUE, calc_gmm_orig = FALSE, calc_gmm_transfr = TRUE,
  dataset_label = "NCBI \u2014 zeros and singletons removed", bins = 20
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  GISAID_log_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 GISAID, zeros removed, Log",
  table_path("Log_GISAID_no0S.html"), important_sites_gisaid
)

save_transformed_comparison_table(
  NCBI_log_no0S,
  "Site selection \u2014 NCBI, zeros and singletons removed, Log",
  table_path("Log_NCBI_no0S.html"), important_sites_ncbi
)

10 Summary: % Overlap Across All Transformations

The tables below consolidate the percentage of VOC reference sites recovered by each GMM subset across all transformations. Cell colour intensity is proportional to overlap — deeper green indicates higher recovery. The maximum value in each transformation column is shown in bold.

10.1 GISAID summary table

gisaid_results <- list(
  "Untransformed"    = GISAID_gmm_no0S,
  "Log1p"            = GISAID_log1p_no0S,
  "Log1p\u2218Log1p" = GISAID_loglog_no0S,
  "Square root"      = GISAID_sqrt_no0S,
  "Reciprocal"       = GISAID_recip_no0S,
  "Box-Cox"          = GISAID_bc_no0S,
  "Log"              = GISAID_log_no0S
)

tbl_summary_gisaid <- generate_summary_table(
  results_list    = gisaid_results,
  important_sites = important_sites_gisaid,
  table_caption   = "Summary: % overlap with GISAID VOC reference sites (Alpha / Beta / Gamma / Delta / Omicron) by GMM subset and transformation",
  save_path       = table_path("Summary_GISAID.html")
)
tbl_summary_gisaid
Summary: % overlap with GISAID VOC reference sites (Alpha / Beta / Gamma / Delta / Omicron) by GMM subset and transformation
Cell values: % overlap with VOC reference sites. Colour intensity proportional to overlap percentage.
Subset Untransformed Log1p Log1p∘Log1p Square root Reciprocal Box-Cox Log
Complete 62.5% 62.5% 76.6% 51.6% 0.0% 51.6% 51.6%
Top 75% 62.5% 62.5% 62.5% 51.6% 0.0% 51.6% 51.6%
Top 50% 62.5% 62.5% 62.5% 51.6% 1.6% 51.6% 51.6%
Top 25% 53.1% 51.6% 51.6% 51.6% 0.0% 51.6% 51.6%
Mean+ 26.6% 26.6% 26.6% 26.6% 1.6% 26.6% 26.6%
90th pct+ 26.6% 26.6% 26.6% 26.6% 12.5% 26.6% 26.6%

10.2 NCBI summary table

ncbi_results <- list(
  "Untransformed"    = NCBI_gmm_no0S,
  "Log1p"            = NCBI_log1p_no0S,
  "Log1p\u2218Log1p" = NCBI_loglog_no0S,
  "Square root"      = NCBI_sqrt_no0S,
  "Reciprocal"       = NCBI_recip_no0S,
  "Box-Cox"          = NCBI_bc_no0S,
  "Log"              = NCBI_log_no0S
)

tbl_summary_ncbi <- generate_summary_table(
  results_list    = ncbi_results,
  important_sites = important_sites_ncbi,
  table_caption   = "Summary: % overlap with NCBI VOC reference sites (Alpha / Beta / Gamma / Delta) by GMM subset and transformation",
  save_path       = table_path("Summary_NCBI.html")
)

References

Cover, Thomas M., and Joy A. Thomas. 2006. Elements of Information Theory. 2nd ed. Wiley.
Fraley, Chris, and Adrian E. Raftery. 2002. “Model-Based Clustering, Discriminant Analysis, and Density Estimation.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 97 (458): 611–31.
Hennig, Christian. 2015. “What Are the True Clusters?” Pattern Recognition Letters 64: 53–62. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patrec.2015.04.009.
Korber, Bette, Will M. Fischer, Sandrasegaram Gnanakaran, et al. 2020. “Tracking Changes in SARS-CoV-2 Spike: Evidence That D614G Increases Infectivity of the COVID-19 Virus.” Cell 182 (4): 812–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2020.06.043.
McLachlan, Geoffrey, and David Peel. 2000. Finite Mixture Models. Wiley.
Shannon, Claude E. 1948. “A Mathematical Theory of Communication.” Bell System Technical Journal 27 (3): 379–423. https://doi.org/10.1002/j.1538-7305.1948.tb01338.x.
